stations.label: THE CURRENCY REFORM
stations.line: They handed me to a broken country on a Sunday, and by Monday the shelves were full — this was not a miracle, it was a decision.
stations.era_photo: A rubble-strewn West German street in June 1948, a queue of subdued civilians in worn wool coats and headscarves outside a distribution office, a man in a modest suit holding a fresh banknote; captured on grainy black-and-white silver gelatin with the harsh contrast and slight blur of postwar press photography.

stations.label: THE BUNDESBANK IS BORN
stations.line: They built me a guardian and made it independent of politicians — one does not leave the vault key with the men who wish to spend.
stations.era_photo: The interior of the newly established Deutsche Bundesbank in Frankfurt, 1957, dark polished wood and modernist glass, officials in grey flannel suits examining ledgers; black-and-white silver gelatin print, formal institutional lighting, sharp and orderly composition.

stations.label: THE FLOAT AGAINST THE DOLLAR
stations.line: When Bretton Woods dissolved, I was cut loose to find my own worth, and the market priced my credibility higher than I had dared to say aloud.
stations.era_photo: A Frankfurt trading floor in March 1973, men in wide-lapel suits and thick-framed glasses shouting over telephones, teletype machines and paper tape everywhere; early color film with the warm, slightly faded Kodachrome palette of the period.

stations.label: THE ONE-TO-ONE CONVERSION
stations.line: They converted the Ostmark to me at parity against my counsel — the risks, I said quietly, had perhaps not been fully considered, and I have carried that compromise ever since.
stations.era_photo: A crowded East German bank in July 1990, citizens in dated 1980s clothing exchanging currency, a wall calendar and portrait removed leaving a pale rectangle; color photography with the muted, slightly overexposed tone of late East German film stock.

stations.label: THE EURO IS DECIDED
stations.line: They agreed to fold me into a larger union, and I insisted the successor inherit my discipline — stability is not a gift, it is a decision, and I refused to let mine be forgotten.
stations.era_photo: A European summit hall in May 1998, finance ministers in dark tailored suits seated at a long table beneath flags, blue conference lighting; crisp early digital press photography, clean and cool in tone.

stations.label: THE LAST DAY IN HAND
stations.line: On the first of the year they gave people euros, and by the end of February the coins that bore my name were no longer legal tender — I did not resist; I had done my work.
stations.era_photo: A German supermarket checkout in early 2002, a cashier's tray holding both Mark coins and new euro coins, shoppers in winter coats; sharp digital color photograph, fluorescent retail lighting, documentary clarity.

stations.label: THE PERMANENT EXCHANGE WINDOW
stations.line: Even in retirement I keep my word — the Bundesbank still honours every Mark note without deadline, because a currency that abandons its promise abandons its meaning.
stations.era_photo: The Bundesbank exchange counter in Frankfurt around 2012, an elderly woman sliding faded Deutsche Mark notes across a glass counter to a clerk, banknote-counting machine visible; high-resolution digital color photograph, even institutional light.

stations.label: STILL COUNTED, STILL KEPT
stations.line: Billions of me remain uncounted in drawers and behind clocks, never returned — we have seen this before: people trust what held the line, long after they were told to let it go.
stations.era_photo: A present-day close-up of aged Deutsche Mark notes and coins laid on a wooden table beside a smartphone showing an exchange app; contemporary high-resolution digital photograph, soft natural window light, shallow depth of field.
